name: .claudeignore Templates
desc: Optimized ignore templates for Node, Python, and Go that prevent Claude from reading lockfiles and noise.
how: Drop a .claudeignore template into your repo root. /setup-ignore auto-detects your stack and generates optimized patterns.
example:
$ /setup-ignore
→ Detected: Node.js + TypeScript
→ Generated .claudeignore (node_modules, dist, *.map)
→ Token cost reduced by ~60%
steps: Review generated patterns and adjust as needed
Verify token savings with uitkit doctor

name: Context Compactor
desc: Synthesizes a massive chat history into a single CLAUDE_STATE.md so users can clear context without losing their place.
how: /save-state compresses your entire session — decisions, edits, architecture — into a portable markdown file.
example:
$ /save-state
→ Analyzing 47 messages, 12 files edited...
→ Created CLAUDE_STATE.md (85 lines)
→ Context compacted! Add @CLAUDE_STATE.md to resume.
steps: Work normally in a long session

name: Caveman Mode
desc: Enforces a strict DSL that forbids conversational English. Strips all chatbot behavior for raw code execution.
how: Activates via /caveman + PreToolUse hook. Claude outputs only structured JSON actions at a fraction of the token cost.
example:
$ /caveman
→ Caveman Mode activated 🦴
→ Output: {"action":"write","file":"auth.ts"}
→ Token savings: 73%
steps: Claude outputs only JSON actions — no filler text

name: Context Pruner
desc: Slash command that prompts Claude to write a session snapshot to .claude/pruned_context.md, then directs the user to wipe memory to avoid token bloat.
how: /prune-context captures all decisions, file edits, and architecture from the current session into a compact markdown file. User then clears context and adds @pruned_context.md to resume.
example:
$ /prune-context
→ Snapshotting 83 messages, 19 files edited...
→ Written .claude/pruned_context.md (120 lines)
→ Now run: /clear && @pruned_context.md
→ Context reduced by 94%. Session resumed.
steps: Add @pruned_context.md to resume with minimal tokens

name: Shadow Compiler
desc: Silently runs the project's compiler after every edit. Catches errors before the task is marked complete.
how: PostToolUse hook fires after every file write. Runs tsc --noEmit, cargo check, or ruff check. Feeds errors back for auto-correction.
steps: Hook auto-fires after every file write
Runs tsc --noEmit or cargo check in background
Errors fed back to Claude for auto-correction

name: Safe Commit Hook
tagline: If it hits GitHub, it works
desc: Forces Claude to run local tests and linters before executing a commit. Blocks dirty code from reaching the repo.
how: Intercepts git commit via PreToolUse hook. Runs test suite, linter, type checker. If any fail, commit is blocked.
example:
$ git commit -m "add auth"
→ Safe Commit: ✓ ESLint  ✗ vitest — 2 failing
→ BLOCKED. Fixing... All passed. Committing.
steps: Install the PreToolUse hook for git commit
Hook runs tests + linter + type check
Commit blocked if any check fails — auto-fix offered

name: Spec-First Enforcer
desc: Reads SPEC.md and physically blocks Claude from executing actions that violate the approved architecture.
how: PreToolUse hook intercepts every Bash/Write. Compares against SPEC.md. Non-compliant actions rejected with citation.
example:
// Claude: ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN email
// Spec Enforcer: ✗ BLOCKED — SPEC.md §4.2: email is immutable.
steps: Create SPEC.md with your architecture rules
Every Bash/Write is intercepted and validated
Non-compliant actions rejected with SPEC.md citation

name: Chaos Monkey
tagline: AI designed to break your code
desc: Adversarial agent swarm that rewrites tests to simulate network drops, race conditions, and massive payloads.
how: /chaos-test spawns adversarial agents that inject failure modes: delays, concurrent writes, null returns, permission errors.
example:
$ /chaos-test src/api/payments.ts
→ 5 chaos agents spawned
→ 2/5 scenarios broke your code. Fix?
steps: Each agent injects different failure modes
Review results and fix broken scenarios

name: Self-Healing CLI Repair Agent
desc: Diagnostics engine that runs tests, captures errors, and compiles self-healing recommendations automatically.
how: uitkit repair runs the full test suite, captures stack traces, identifies root causes, and generates fix suggestions with code patches.
example:
$ uitkit repair
→ Running 142 tests...
→ ✗ 3 failures detected
→ src/auth.ts:42 — TypeError: Cannot read property 'id'
→ Suggested fix: add null check before .id access
→ Patch applied. Re-running... ✓ All 142 passing.
steps: Run uitkit repair in your project root
Full test suite executed with error capture
Stack traces analyzed for root cause

name: Fail-Fast Enforcer
desc: Global guidelines and PostToolUse hook that block execution and error swallowing when shell commands exit with non-zero status.
how: fail-fast.md defines the rule. fail-fast.sh PostToolUse hook intercepts every Bash execution. If exit code ≠ 0, it blocks further execution and forces error handling.
install: npx uitkit add rule fail-fast && npx uitkit add hook fail-fast
steps: Install fail-fast.md rule into your rules directory
Install fail-fast.sh as a PostToolUse hook
Hook intercepts every Bash command execution
Non-zero exit codes block further execution

name: Measure Twice / Plan-First Hook
desc: Rules and PreToolUse validation hook that block file changes or script executions unless a .claude/plan.md has been marked as approved.
how: measure-twice.md defines the planning rule. measure-twice.sh PreToolUse hook checks for .claude/plan.md with APPROVED status before allowing any file write or script execution.
install: npx uitkit add rule measure-twice && npx uitkit add hook measure-twice
steps: Install measure-twice.md rule and measure-twice.sh hook
Mark plan as APPROVED when ready
Hook gates all file writes until plan is approved

name: Grill Me
desc: Refuses to write code until Claude has ruthlessly interrogated the user on edge cases and security.
how: /grill-me activates a Socratic loop. After 10+ rounds of questions, produces a hardened SPEC.md.
example:
$ /grill-me Build a payment webhook
→ Q1: Duplicate Stripe events?
→ Q2: Idempotency key strategy?
→ ... 12 questions → Generated SPEC.md
steps: Proceed to implementation with full spec coverage

name: Stunt Double
desc: Orchestrates a test-engineer agent to write failing tests, then an implementation-engineer to make them pass.
how: /stunt-double spawns two agents sequentially: TestEngineer writes tests, ImplementationEngineer writes minimal code.
example:
$ /stunt-double Add rate limiter
→ Phase 1: 8 failing tests
→ Phase 2: All 8 passing ✓
steps: TestEngineer agent writes failing tests first
ImplementationEngineer writes minimal code to pass

name: Architect / Mason
desc: Expensive model designs the architecture, cheap model implements the boilerplate at minimal cost.
how: /architect uses Opus for ARCHITECTURE.md. /mason uses Haiku to implement each file rapidly.
example:
$ /architect Design notification system
→ ARCHITECTURE.md (200 lines, 5 files)
$ /mason implement → ✓ ($0.03)
steps: Run /mason implement to build each file with Haiku

name: ADR Generator
desc: Generates standardized Architectural Decision Records when major systemic changes are detected.
how: /adr detects architectural pivots and generates ADR files following the Michael Nygard format.
example:
$ /adr → Detected: REST to GraphQL
→ docs/adr/003-graphql-migration.md
  Status: ACCEPTED
steps: Review the generated ADR file in docs/adr/
Accept or modify the decision record
ADRs auto-link to git commits for traceability

name: Blast Radius Analyzer
desc: AST-traces downstream imports before a breaking change, generating a visual impact map.
how: /blast-radius parses the AST, traces import chains, generates a dependency graph of affected files.
example:
$ /blast-radius src/utils/auth.ts
→ 14 files depend on auth.ts
→ 3 critical paths identified
steps: AST parses all import chains downstream
Critical paths highlighted in red — proceed with caution

name: Legacy Strangler
desc: Prevents Big Bang rewrites. Builds facades and extracts microservices safely from legacy monoliths.
how: /strangle analyzes the monolith, identifies bounded contexts, creates a strangler fig plan.
example:
$ /strangle orders.ts (2,400 lines)
→ 4 bounded contexts identified
→ Phase 1: Extracting Order Creation...
steps: Facade layer generated for safe extraction

name: Claude Council Swarm Launcher
desc: Multi-agent system coordinator that spawns domain-specific subagents and outputs tailored domain rules to COUNCIL_INSTRUCTIONS.md.
how: uitkit council <domain> activates a council of specialist agents. Each agent contributes domain expertise. Results synthesized into a unified instruction set.
example:
$ uitkit council fintech
→ Spawning 5 specialist agents...
→ Compliance Agent: PCI-DSS rules drafted
→ Security Agent: encryption standards defined
→ Architecture Agent: microservice patterns set
→ Written COUNCIL_INSTRUCTIONS.md (180 lines)
steps: 5 specialist agents spawned for your domain

name: Swarm Sandbox Simulator
desc: Interactive visualization UI for agent teams and swarm simulation. Watch agents coordinate, delegate, and resolve tasks in real-time.
how: Opens the Swarm window in the UitKit OS dashboard. Renders a live simulation of agent orchestration with task graphs, delegation chains, and progress tracking.
example:
→ Open Swarm window from desktop
→ Select: Hive Orchestrator pattern
→ 6 agents spawned: backend, frontend, security, QA, docs, devops
→ Live task graph: 12 tasks, 3 dependencies
→ Simulation complete: all tasks green ✓
steps: Run uitkit dashboard to open the OS interface
Open the Swarm window from the desktop
Select a swarm pattern: Hive, Night Shift, or Tribunal
Watch agents coordinate in real-time with task graphs

name: Night Shift
desc: Queues hundreds of files and manages its own API rate limits via notification hooks.
how: Creates BATCH_QUEUE.md, processes each file, marks done, handles rate limits. Designed for 3+ hour unsupervised sessions.
example:
$ /night-shift Migrate .js to TypeScript
→ 50 files queued
→ 1/50: auth.js → auth.ts ✓
→ Rate limit hit. Sleeping 60s...
steps: Each file processed sequentially with rate-limit handling
Check BATCH_QUEUE.md for progress — designed for 3+ hour sessions

name: Hive Orchestrator
desc: Master routing agent that breaks epics into sub-tasks and spawns specialized subagents.
how: /hive-swarm decomposes an epic into a task graph, assigns to specialist agents, orchestrates with dependency management.
example:
$ /hive-swarm Build user dashboard
→ 6 tasks: backend, frontend, security, integration
→ Spawning agents...
steps: Epic decomposed into task graph with dependencies
Specialist agents assigned to each task
Agents execute in parallel with dependency management

name: Tribunal Review
desc: Spawns Hacker, Performance Junkie, and Senior Pedant to audit a PR from every angle.
how: Orchestrator spawns three adversarial personas. Each reviews the same diff. Results synthesized into one review.
example:
$ /tribunal auth.js
⚖️ 🛡️ Hacker: Timing attack L42
⚡ Perf: N+1 query in lookup
📐 Pedant: console.log L88
steps: 3 adversarial personas spawned: Hacker, Perf Junkie, Pedant
Each reviews the same diff independently
Results synthesized into one unified review

name: Codebase Sweeper
desc: Low-cost background agent that continuously hunts dead code, unused imports, and stale comments.
how: /sweep runs AST analysis to find unused exports, dead imports, resolved TODOs. Removes silently.
example:
$ /sweep → Scanning 340 files...
→ 23 dead imports, 8 TODOs, 4 unused exports
→ Cleaned 35 items. -200 lines of debt.
steps: AST analysis scans all files for dead code
Unused imports, exports, and TODOs identified
Items cleaned automatically — review the diff

name: Time-Travel Debugger
desc: Writes a test script and jumps through Git history to find the exact commit that broke the code.
how: Writes deterministic test, runs git bisect, identifies offending commit, reads diff, proposes fix.
example:
$ /bisect-bug Login returns 500
→ git bisect through 47 commits...
→ Found! Commit f9e8d7c: removed await before DB call
steps: Git bisect jumps through commit history

name: Auto-TDD Hook
desc: Watches for file saves and runs the corresponding test file in the background, feeding results to Claude.
how: PostToolUse hook maps source files to test files. When src/auth.ts is saved, auto-runs tests/auth.test.ts.
steps: Hook maps source files to test files automatically
When src/auth.ts is saved, tests/auth.test.ts runs
Results fed back to Claude for instant feedback

name: Dev Doctor
desc: Diagnoses ECONNREFUSED errors by checking Docker, port conflicts, .env drift, and common issues.
how: /dev-doctor runs a diagnostic checklist: Docker? Port? .env? Node version? Database?
example:
$ /dev-doctor
→ ✓ Node v20.11  ✗ Port 3000 occupied
→ ✗ .env missing DATABASE_URL
→ 3 issues. Auto-fix? [Y/n]
steps: Diagnostic checklist runs: Docker, ports, .env, Node
Accept fixes and re-run to verify

name: JIT Context Injector
desc: Dynamically finds and injects downstream dependency signatures before Claude edits a core file.
how: PreToolUse hook detects target file, AST-traces imports, extracts type signatures, injects as context.
steps: Hook detects target file before every edit
AST traces imports and extracts type signatures
Signatures injected as context for Claude

name: Constitution Guardrail
desc: Physically blocks code that violates CONSTITUTION.md. Turns guidelines into architectural enforcements.
how: PreToolUse hook reads CONSTITUTION.md, intercepts every code write. Violations are rejected with a citation to the violated article.
steps: Create CONSTITUTION.md with your team guidelines
Every code write is checked against your articles
Violations rejected with citation to the violated article

name: The Auditor
desc: Side-by-side comparison of final code against the original SPEC.md. Guarantees AI didn't go rogue.
how: /audit-spec compares every implementation file against SPEC.md sections. Generates a compliance report with deviations.
example:
$ /audit-spec
→ Comparing 12 files vs SPEC.md...
→ 11/12 compliant
→ Deviation: auth.ts §2.1 — missing rate limit
→ Fix deviation?
steps: Every file compared against SPEC.md sections

name: Ghost in the Machine
desc: Background routine that generates a refactored 'ideal' version of recent edits. Non-intrusive architectural mentoring.
how: PostToolUse routine watches edits, generates a shadow branch with cleaner patterns. Review at your leisure.
steps: Review shadow patches at your leisure

name: The Interrogator
desc: Comments on vague GitHub/Linear tickets to extract reproduction steps automatically.
how: /triage-issue reads the ticket, identifies missing info, and posts a structured comment asking for repro steps, environment, logs.
example:
$ /triage-issue #342
→ Analyzing: 'Login is broken'
→ Missing: browser, OS, error logs, steps
→ Posted comment: 4 clarifying questions
steps: Structured comment posted with clarifying questions
Repro steps, environment, and logs requested

name: The Archaeologist
desc: Local vector-based search for conceptual code finding. Instant onboarding into massive codebases.
how: /find-concept builds a local embedding index of the repo. Query by concept, not just keyword.
example:
$ /find-concept "how do we handle auth tokens?"
→ Found 8 relevant files across 3 modules
→ Top: src/auth/jwt.ts (score: 0.94)
steps: Local embedding index built from your repo
Files ranked by conceptual relevance score
Navigate to top results for instant onboarding

name: Specify Wizard
desc: Interactive skill that generates GitHub-compliant specs using Grill Me logic. Automates formal engineering.
how: /specify walks through structured questions, generates SPEC.md, ARCHITECTURE.md, and ADR in one session.
example:
$ /specify New payment gateway
→ 15 questions answered
→ Generated: SPEC.md, ARCH.md, ADR-004
→ Ready for /mason implement
steps: Answer 15 structured questions about your feature

name: Privacy-First Telemetry Opt-in
desc: Secure onboarding configuration step in uitkit init with settings state persistence. Users explicitly opt-in to any data collection.
how: During uitkit init, a dedicated step presents telemetry options. Settings persisted to .claude/settings.json. Zero data collected without explicit opt-in.
example:
$ uitkit init
→ Step 4/5: Telemetry Settings
→ [ ] Anonymous usage statistics
→ [ ] Error reporting
→ [ ] Performance metrics
→ None selected by default. Opt-in? [y/N]
→ Settings saved to .claude/settings.json
steps: Run uitkit init to start onboarding
Select which data categories to opt-in

name: Executive HTML Compliance Audit
desc: Generates strict compliance audit logs in HTML format for SOC2/GDPR auditing. Produces a professional uitkit-audit-report.html.
how: uitkit audit --html scans the project against SOC2, GDPR, and EU AI Act frameworks. Generates a styled HTML report with pass/fail matrices and remediation steps.
example:
$ uitkit audit --html
→ Scanning 8 compliance dimensions...
→ ✓ Access Control (SOC2 CC6.1)
→ ✗ Data Retention (GDPR Art.17) — 2 violations
→ ✓ Encryption at Rest (SOC2 CC6.7)
→ Generated uitkit-audit-report.html (24 pages)

name: Model Permission Editor
desc: Interactive command manager for listing, granting, or revoking auto-execution permissions for Claude Code tools and actions.
how: uitkit permissions opens an interactive editor showing all tool permissions. Grant or revoke auto-execution for Bash, file writes, MCP servers, and network access.
example:
$ uitkit permissions
→ Current permissions (14 rules):
  ✓ Bash: allowed (npm, git, node)
  ✗ Bash: denied (rm, sudo, curl)
  ✓ FileWrite: allowed (*.ts, *.js)
  ✗ Network: denied (external APIs)
→ Toggle [Bash:denied] → grant? [y/N]
steps: Review current permission rules (Bash, FileWrite, Network)
Toggle allow/deny for each tool category
Changes saved immediately — no restart needed

name: CLAUDE.md Sentinel
desc: CLI rules scanner and writer that automatically writes or updates anti-hallucination project rules inside CLAUDE.md.
how: uitkit sentinel scans project patterns, common hallucination risks, and team conventions. Writes defensive rules directly into CLAUDE.md to prevent AI from guessing.
example:
$ uitkit sentinel
→ Scanning project structure...
→ Detected: TypeScript + Prisma + Express
→ Writing 8 anti-hallucination rules to CLAUDE.md:
  - Never guess Prisma schema fields
  - Always verify import paths exist
  - Check API route definitions before writing handlers
→ CLAUDE.md updated with sentinel rules.
steps: Project structure scanned for hallucination risks
Rules updated on each run — safe to re-run

name: The Historian
desc: Background hook that watches for edits in core directories and queues automatic ARCHITECTURE.md rewrites.
how: PostToolUse hook monitors critical paths. When src/api/* or src/db/* changes, updates ARCHITECTURE.md.
steps: Hook monitors src/api/* and src/db/* changes
ARCHITECTURE.md auto-updated when core files change

name: Sonar Codebase Cartographer
desc: AST/signature extraction for massive repos. Gives Claude an exact map instead of guessing from context.
how: /map-repo builds an AST index of all public APIs, types, and relationships. Stores in .uitkit/map.json.
example:
$ /map-repo
→ Scanning 2,400 files...
→ Indexed: 340 APIs, 180 types, 90 interfaces
→ Context map ready. Claude now has full repo vision.
steps: Run uitkit map or uitkit chart
AST scans all files for public APIs and types
Claude now has full repo vision for precise context

name: The Prophet
desc: Analyzes Git hotspots to predict which files will cause the next production outage.
how: /prophet correlates git blame density, change frequency, and test coverage to predict fragile files.
example:
$ /prophet
→ Analyzing 6 months of git history...
→ 🔴 HIGH RISK: src/api/payments.ts (47 changes, 30% coverage)
→ 🟡 MEDIUM: src/utils/auth.ts (22 changes)
steps: Run uitkit prophet in your project root
Files ranked by risk score (change frequency x low coverage)

name: Invariant Discovery
desc: Analyzes Git history to extract unwritten team conventions — the rules of the road that aren't documented.
how: /discover-invariants scans commit patterns, PR reviews, and code style to surface implicit team agreements.
example:
$ /discover-invariants
→ 3 months of history analyzed
→ Found 12 invariants:
  1. Always use async/await (never .then)
  2. DB queries always in repositories/
  3. No console.log in production code
steps: Unwritten conventions extracted from commit patterns
Add discovered invariants to CLAUDE.md or CONSTITUTION.md

name: The Oracle
desc: Simulates edge cases against a PR to predict specific production failure modes before merge.
how: /oracle-pr reads the diff, generates adversarial test scenarios: high load, race conditions, null inputs.
example:
$ /oracle-pr #128
→ Simulating 15 edge cases...
→ 🔴 FAIL: 10K concurrent users → deadlock in order.ts
→ 🟡 WARN: null response from payment API
steps: Race conditions, null inputs, high load tested

name: Graph-Augmented Context
desc: Maps the repository as a Knowledge Graph instead of a flat file list. Understands inheritance and data flow.
how: /build-graph creates a SQLite-backed knowledge graph of code relationships. Claude queries it for precise context.
example:
$ /build-graph
→ Building knowledge graph...
→ 1,200 nodes, 3,400 edges
→ Claude: "Show me all handlers that touch User"
→ 8 handlers found across 4 modules
steps: Run /build-graph to create the knowledge graph
SQLite-backed graph of all code relationships
Query with natural language: "Show me all handlers that touch User"

name: Recursive Reflection
desc: PostToolUse hook that forces Claude to reflect on its own code by running it against a Judge model.
how: After every tool use, a lightweight Judge model reviews the output. If issues found, Claude self-corrects before returning.
steps: Judge model reviews every tool output
Issues flagged and sent back for self-correction
Final output validated before returning to user

name: SVG Interactive Map Inspector
desc: High-performance SVG visualization module in the dashboard GUI to browse file nodes, inspect dependencies, and explore the codebase interactively.
how: Accessible via the Toolkit → Codebase Map tab. Renders an interactive SVG graph of all project files. Click any node to inspect its functions, imports, and downstream dependencies.
example:
→ Open Toolkit → Codebase Map
→ 10 nodes rendered across 3 groups (CLI, Core, Website)
→ Click: cli.js → 4 functions, 4 imports
→ Highlighted: 3 downstream dependencies
→ Inspector panel: initCommand(), doctorCommand()...
steps: Run uitkit dashboard to open the OS interface
Open Toolkit, then click the Codebase Map tab
Click any node to inspect its functions and imports

name: Architect / Mason Federation
desc: Expensive model designs, cheap model builds — orchestrated across multiple files with dependency tracking.
how: /federation takes an epic, Architect creates full blueprint, Mason agents implement each file in parallel.
example:
$ /federation Build notification system
→ Architect: 8 files planned, dependency graph ready
→ Mason agents: 8 parallel implementations
→ All done in 45 seconds ($0.12 total)
steps: Architect model creates full blueprint with dependency graph
Mason agents implement each file in parallel

name: Cross-Talk
desc: Allows a local Claude session to query a colleague's Claude session. Collective team hive-mind.
how: /cross-talk connects to a teammate's session via MCP. Query their context, share findings, coordinate.
example:
$ /cross-talk @alice "What auth pattern are you using?"
→ Alice's session: JWT with refresh tokens, RSA-256
→ Shall I adopt the same pattern?
steps: Teammate's Claude session responds with context
Adopt or adapt their patterns in your codebase

name: DBA-in-a-Box
desc: Uses MCP to run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on a replica before writing migrations. Prevents bad queries hitting prod.
how: PreToolUse hook intercepts migration files. Runs EXPLAIN ANALYZE on replica. Blocks queries with full table scans.
steps: Write a migration file as normal
Hook runs EXPLAIN ANALYZE on replica automatically
Blocks bad queries and suggests index improvements

name: Incident Commander
desc: Wakes up on PagerDuty alerts, reads logs, rolls back deployments, drafts post-mortems.
how: Webhook triggers on PagerDuty alert. Reads CloudWatch/Datadog logs, identifies root cause, executes rollback playbook.
example:
⏰ PagerDuty: 500 errors spike
→ Incident Commander activated
→ Reading logs... found: deployment abc123
→ Rolling back to previous version...
→ Drafting post-mortem...
steps: Agent reads logs, identifies root cause, executes rollback

name: Self-Healing CI Pipeline
desc: GitHub Action that automatically patches and pushes fixes for failing CI tests.
how: On CI failure, spawns a repair agent that reads the error, patches the code, pushes a fix commit, re-triggers CI.
example:
CI: ✗ test_auth.py — AssertionError
→ Self-Healing CI activated
→ Agent: updated expected value (line 42)
→ Pushed fix commit → CI: ✓ All passing
steps: Add the self-healing-ci GitHub Action to your workflow
On CI failure, repair agent spawns automatically
Agent reads error, patches code, pushes fix commit
CI re-triggers and validates the fix

name: MCP Dynamic Discovery
desc: Scans the user's system for available MCP servers and automatically wires them into the active session.
how: /discover-mcp scans localhost, Docker, and common ports. Auto-generates MCP configs for found services.
example:
$ /discover-mcp
→ Scanning system...
→ Found: PostgreSQL (5432), Redis (6379), Figma MCP
→ Auto-generated 3 MCP configs
→ Connected and ready
steps: Connect and use discovered services in your session

name: Vibe & Verify
desc: Vibe-Maker builds UI, Skeptic audits for accessibility, responsiveness, and edge cases. Polished pixels guaranteed.
how: /vibe spawns two agents: Vibe-Maker generates React components, Skeptic runs WCAG audit and mobile-responsiveness checks.
example:
$ /vibe Build a pricing page
→ Vibe-Maker: 3-tier pricing cards ✨
→ Skeptic: ✗ Mobile overflow at 320px
→ Vibe-Maker: Fixed. ✓ WCAG AA ✓ Responsive
steps: Skeptic agent audits for WCAG and responsiveness

name: Figma-to-Code Bridge
desc: Uses Figma MCP to autonomously build React components from JSON design trees.
how: /figma-to-code reads Figma design tree via MCP, maps to React components, generates styled JSX with Tailwind.
example:
$ /figma-to-code Frame "Hero Section"
→ Reading Figma tree... 12 layers detected
→ Generated: HeroSection.tsx (48 lines)
→ Tailwind classes applied ✓  Responsive ✓
steps: Ensure Figma MCP server is running
Claudent reads Figma design tree via MCP
Generated React component with Tailwind classes ready

name: The Artifact
desc: Every prompt generates a versioned Artifact that acts as single source of truth. Code is just a renderable output.
how: /artifact creates a Spec Kit-compatible versioned document. Edit the Artifact → UitKit regenerates the code.
example:
$ /artifact Build user settings page
→ Generated Artifact v1.0 (spec + acceptance criteria)
→ Code rendered from Artifact
→ Business change? Edit Artifact → Code regenerates
steps: Review generated spec and acceptance criteria
Code renders from the Artifact as single source of truth
Edit Artifact to regenerate code on requirement changes

name: Atomic Commit Hook
desc: Auto-commits successfully tested code after every AI edit. Instant rollbacks without untangling files.
how: PostToolUse hook: after tests pass, creates a micro-commit. If the next edit breaks things, git revert to last good state.
steps: After tests pass, micro-commit created automatically
On test failure, auto-revert to last good commit

name: Design System Extraction
desc: Extracts design tokens from Figma/designs, generates component libraries and theme mappings.
how: /extract-tokens reads Figma file, extracts colors, spacing, typography, shadows. Generates CSS variables + Tailwind config.
example:
$ /extract-tokens Figma URL
→ Extracted: 24 colors, 8 spacing, 6 typography
→ Generated: tokens.css + tailwind.extend.js
→ Theme mapping: 100% coverage
steps: Apply theme mapping across your component library

name: The Pulse Statusline
desc: Live statusline showing swarm status, context budget, session cost, and map density on the Claude terminal floor.
how: Installs as a statusline config. Renders real-time metrics with color-coded indicators.
steps: Statusline renders at terminal bottom in real-time
Color-coded alerts for budget and context thresholds

name: The Matrix Theme Pack
desc: Brand-aligned high-contrast themes: UitKit Neon, Ghost Shell. For senior power users.
how: Install via /theme matrix-neon. Applies color scheme to statusline, terminal, and Claude Code UI.
example:
$ /theme matrix-neon
→ Applied: UitKit Neon theme
→ Terminal: green-on-black with syntax highlighting
→ Statusline: matching neon accents
steps: Theme applies to statusline, terminal, and UI
Syntax highlighting updates across all panes

name: Power-User Keybindings
desc: Shortcut map for high-frequency skills: Doctor, Save-State, Grill-Me. Professional AI engineer ergonomics.
how: Install keybindings.json. Maps Ctrl+D to doctor, Ctrl+S to save-state, Ctrl+G to grill-me.
example:
Ctrl+D → uitkit doctor (instant)
Ctrl+G → /grill-me (interrogation mode)
Ctrl+Shift+S → /save-state (compact & resume)
steps: Restart Claude Code to load new shortcuts
Ctrl+D triggers doctor, Ctrl+G triggers grill-me

name: High-Speed Shell Aliases
desc: Short-circuit CLI commands: cx, cxd, cxa. Reduces friction and reinforces UitKit as a platform.
how: Add to .bashrc/.zshrc. cx = uitkit, cxd = uitkit doctor, cxa = uitkit audit.
example:
$ cx doctor   (≡ uitkit doctor)
$ cx score    (≡ uitkit score)
$ cxa         (≡ uitkit audit --full)
steps: Run setup to append aliases to .bashrc/.zshrc
Use cx, cxd, cxa as short commands

name: Offline GUI Desktop Dashboard
desc: Launchable local web dashboard interface for playground stacks and harness-neutral downloads. Run uitkit dashboard to open the full OS experience.
how: uitkit dashboard starts a local web server and opens the UitKit OS interface in your browser. Access all windows: CLI, Toolkit, Showcase, Swarm, and more.
example:
$ uitkit dashboard
→ Starting local server...
→ ✓ Server running at http://localhost:4321
→ Opening UitKit OS desktop...
→ 16 windows available: Skills, Agents, Stacks, CLI...
→ Drag windows, open apps, explore the ecosystem.
steps: Run uitkit dashboard from any project
OS desktop opens with 16 draggable windows
Explore CLI, Toolkit, Showcase, Swarm, and more

name: TDD Workflow Skill
desc: Enforces strict Test-Driven Development execution sequence: write failing test, code minimal features, refactor code.
how: Enforced automatically during developer agent execution loops to ensure 100% test validation.
example:
$ uitkit add skill tdd-workflow
→ Stage 1: Red test added
→ Stage 2: Green minimal code written
→ Stage 3: Code refactored
steps: Write a failing test case first
Implement minimal code to pass tests
Refactor and verify tests stay green

name: Glassmorphism UI System
desc: Provides style guidelines and utility parameters for frosted glass container panels using backdrop blurs and subtle borders.
how: Renders layout parameters to align CSS backdrop blurs and transparent gradients.

name: Responsive CSS Grids
desc: Layout guidelines and column setups for fluid grid systems that scale gracefully from mobile to desktop sizes.
how: Configures media queries and grid repeaters to automatically align layout structures.
steps: Scale columns up inside responsive breakpoints

name: Marketing Funnel Auditor
desc: Diagnostic checklist to audit conversion metrics and dropoffs across Top, Middle, and Bottom of the customer funnel.
how: Evaluates ad click-through rates, trial signup conversions, and checkout friction points.
example:
$ uitkit add skill marketing-funnel-analysis
→ TOFU Leak: 3.2% ad CTR detected
→ MOFU Leak: 85% signup dropoff
→ BOFU Leak: 40% bounce on pricing page
steps: Audit awareness channels and CTR metrics (TOFU)
Check visitor conversion to lead signups (MOFU)
Optimize headlines and user onboarding flows

name: Mixture of Experts Router
desc: Intelligent orchestrator agent that parses developer tasks and delegates parts to Opus, Sonnet, and Haiku for maximum speed and cost efficiency.
how: Interprets request complexity to build and trigger model execution blueprints.
example:
Plan Task -> Opus
Write Boilerplate -> Haiku
Assemble -> Sonnet
steps: Classify request complexity and risk levels
Build and write boilerplate using Haiku

name: SOLID Design Principles
desc: Enforces the five core SOLID rules during code review and refactoring for scalable, modular class design.
how: Inspects code coupling and splits multi-purpose functions to comply with single responsibility bounds.
example: Class UserManager -> split into UserRepo + UserAuthenticator
steps: Inject abstract dependencies instead of concretions (DIP)

name: Domain-Driven Design
desc: Models complex logic around bounded contexts, defining aggregates, value objects, and repository boundaries.
how: Filters database entities from leaking into core logic, ensuring boundaries are accessed only via aggregate roots.
example: Aggregate Root: Order -> Item adds and validates pricing total invariants
steps: Define core domain entities and value objects
Abstract data access through Domain Repositories
Decouple frameworks from business domain logic

name: Hexagonal Architecture
desc: Decouples core business logic (Inner Ring) from infrastructure (Outer Ring) using Inbound and Outbound Ports.
how: Exposes interfaces (Ports) inside domain, implementing database/web handlers outside (Adapters).
steps: Construct domain logic without outside dependencies
Create Inbound and Outbound Port interfaces
Implement primary adapters for delivery hooks (REST/CLI)
Implement secondary adapters for infrastructure hooks (DB/API)

name: Strangler Fig Migration
desc: Deconstruct monolith codebases into microservices iteratively using the Strangler Fig pattern.
how: Introduces a facade layer to intercept and redirect target endpoints to the new microservice, keeping legacy routing as fallback.
example: Facade Layer -> redirects /orders to orders-service, falls back to legacy-monolith for other routes
steps: Identify the target domain context for extraction
Build a facade/interception layer at the API boundary
Deploy new microservice with isolated database
Route subset traffic to new service

name: Saga Distributed Transactions
desc: Coordinate distributed transactions across multiple microservices using Saga Orchestration or Choreography patterns.
how: Runs a sequence of local transactions, publishing events to trigger subsequent steps. Executes reverse compensating transactions on failure.
steps: Implement execution state tracking for steps
Define forward transactions for each target service
Write compensating undo transactions for all steps
Ensure strict idempotency across all action handlers

name: CQRS Segregation
desc: Segregate read (Query) operations from write (Command) operations to optimize performance, scaling, and database mappings.
how: Maintains completely distinct handlers and schemas for state mutations (Commands) and state representation (Queries).
example: CreateOrderCommand (inserts relational OLTP) vs GetOrderDetailsQuery (fetches Elasticsearch denormalized view)
steps: Segregate write mutations into Command classes
Segregate data retrievals into Query classes
Use separate DB connections/replicas for read/write
Construct custom schemas optimized for layout views

name: Transactional Outbox
desc: Implement the Transactional Outbox pattern to guarantee reliable message publishing and event-driven data consistency.
how: Saves both entity state updates and event payloads in the same database transaction, processed by an async publisher.
example: DB transaction -> INSERT order AND INSERT outbox_event -> background worker pushes to Kafka
steps: Create outbox table inside primary database schema
Write event payloads in same transactional context as data write
Configure background poller/CDC worker to read outbox table
Mark outbox records as processed to keep footprint small

name: Circuit Breaker
desc: Protect systems from cascading failures by wrapping external integrations in a stateful Circuit Breaker helper.
how: Monitors remote request failure rates. Instantly blocks calls (CLOSED -> OPEN) when degraded, periodically testing recovery (HALF-OPEN).
example: Call Payment Gateway (fails 5 times) -> Breaker OPENS -> returns fallback response immediately
steps: Establish sliding failure window counter limits
Monitor remote API call outcomes inside CLOSED state
Fail fast with fallback data inside OPEN state
Verify system recovery before closing circuit breaker
